case_name: State of New Hampshire v. Max Giroux

segments.text: This is case 2024, 0505, state of New Hampshire versus Max Giroux.
Good morning for the record.
Justice Will is unable to be with us physically present today, but he is going to participate in our deliberations having reviewed recording of the argument.
Tony Feeland.
Good morning. I have reserved two minutes.
May it please the court? My name is Pamela Feeland. I'm here today on behalf of Mr. Giroux.
There are three points I'd like to address this morning.
First, the prior uncharged and isolated act was not part of a clearly discernible plan to groom the complainant in this case.
Two, evidence about the child advocacy interview process improperly bolstered the credibility of the complainant's allegations.
And lastly, the error in admitting both types of evidence was not harmless beyond a reasonable doubt.

Regarding the prior act evidence, discreet and isolated acts with no demonstrated connection to the charged acts are not acts that are part of a calculated plan to commit later assaults and are not admissible as grooming evidence.
This court has been very clear in all the cases it has issued addressing the admissibility of alleged grooming evidence.
There has to be a sequence of acts that demonstrate a discernible plan on the behalf of the defendant.
That a sequence of acts resembles a design when examined in retrospect is not enough.
The prior conduct must be intertwined with what follows such that the prior conduct and the charged act were mutually dependent.
That's a quote from the State v. Melcher case.
The defining characteristics that this court has focused on in these cases involving alleged grooming evidence include whether there are more than one act,
what is the temporal relationship between the single act or acts and the charged act.
Here, there was at least a year period of time between one single act that was admitted as grooming evidence and the charged acts that came later.
Interestingly enough, in all the grooming evidence cases that I've looked at from this court, the complainant in the case is the one who has talked about in testimony
the alleged grooming acts and how it interrelated with the charged acts.
In this case, that is not the evidence. The complainant in this case wasn't even asked about the prior act or how she viewed it in relation to the charged act or otherwise.
So we don't know whether she remembered that ever happening?
Correct. There was no evidence that the charged acts hinged on would not have happened or were mutually dependent on this single isolated act that a third party testified about.
And again, there was no particular temporal connection between the charged act, acts, and that prior act.
Interestingly enough, the state didn't even articulate in arguments to the jury how that prior act made it possible for the charged acts to happen,
how that prior act was part of a discernible plan by the defendant. Even the state didn't make that argument.
Had they made that argument, wouldn't that have made it more prejudicial?
It certainly would have made it more prejudicial, but I think even standing alone, the evidence without that argument was prejudicial.
Not only was a third party witness allowed to testify about this incident that happened at least a year prior to the charged acts,
but that same witness was allowed to talk about how the incident worried her.
That was the only testimony that this witness offered during the trial.
Collectively, that evidence conveyed to the jury that there was something nefarious about that prior incident.
That again, the complainant didn't even testify about.
And that was the only act that was actually corroborated by a witness of all of these acts at issue in this case.
That was the only act that was witnessed by a third party.
Yes, so the charged acts, there was absolutely no corroborating evidence, correct.
And this witness in fact didn't even see the incident at issue in the prior act.
That witness heard someone describe what that person saw,
and the person who actually witnessed what or was not happening in that bedroom was a third party who did not even testify.
Didn't he deny that he ever saw that?
Counsel in arguing about the admissibility of the evidence indicated that witness would deny that he made this statement.
He did not testify at trial.
But yes, that was discussed at the bench conference.
But Mr. Juro was portrayed as a father figure to the complainant, right?
Correct.
So isn't that sort of benign that any parent with a sick child would go into that child's bedroom
and lay down with the child until the child falls asleep at seven countless times in my family?
It is, it can be an innocuous parental action that is completely understandable.
But that is not the evidence that came in.
The evidence that came in was describing what the witness heard about Mr. Garo being in the bedroom, in the bed,
and then she coupled it with her characterization of it causing her to be worried.
This is a person, a witness who knew Mr. Garo at the time, who had a family connection to Mr. Garo,
and it certainly allowed the jury to speculate and suggest to the jury that she had a reason not to think that this was innocuous,
but that it was worse.
Why isn't it harmless?
It's not harmless because in this case it was clear that the jury was struggling with the only evidence of the charged acts,
which was the complainant's testimony.
The complainant in this case, around the time that she now alleges the acts happened at the time,
she was twice asked whether Mr. Garo had done anything to her inappropriate and she denied it.
It wasn't until years later that she made her allegations.
The jury deliberated in this case for a total of I think five hours or five and a half hours,
after three and a half hours, and that was over the course of two days.
After three and a half hours, the jury reported to the court that they had reached an impasse.
The judge gave further instructions to the jury and after another two hours, the jury the next day came back with its verdict.
Do we have cases where we've assessed harmless error and we've looked at the length of time of deliberation
or the jury being at an impasse at one point or another?
I can't say off the top of my head whether that has specifically been a factor that the court has looked at,
but I would say that that indicates that the jury was struggling with whether the state had been able to meet its burden of proof
with the evidence that was admitted about the charged acts.
The case rested solely on the testimony of the complainant.
She had made prior inconsistent statements denying the allegations
and she was strenuously attacked through cross examination
and throughout other cross examination of other witnesses about her story about the charged acts.
And this court has looked at the fact when assessing harmlessness,
what is the weight of the evidence, the other evidence in the case, what was the strength of the state's case?
And how often did the state reference the evidence that was erroneously admitted
and here you agree that the state did not reference it in their closing argument?
But I think that also indicates that this was evidence that was put in
that even the state knew wasn't tied to or was an event that the charged acts were mutually dependent upon it.
And that evidence, again, this is a witness that's her sole testimony,
was to come in and to talk about this incident and talk about how it worried her.
That's kind of an anomaly in the rest of the case and that's the only thing that witness testified about.
And given the collective, given the entirety of her testimony,
it's more akin, I think, to bad character evidence.
Her characterization of it being a worrisome event and then testifying about this event.
Someone who knew Mr. Garot, who was in the house around the time period,
you know, about a year before the charged acts.
That all strikes me as character evidence, bad character evidence.
With your light on, I'd like to hear about the CAC issue.
Yeah, I agree.
I mean, maybe I'm disappointed I never thought about this argument,
but doesn't this evidence come in all the time in these cases regarding the CAC?
I don't think so.
Well, does it come in?
I would say at varying degrees, it does come in.
Do I think it's appropriate?
No, as I've argued in my brief.
The, to some extent, the state is given leeway in its case in chief
to outline investigative steps it takes during an investigation.
But importantly in this case, there was no dispute that the police properly investigated this case.
There was no dispute in this case that the allegations that the complainant was making were suggested to her
or was some sort of suggested memory from an improper interview process.
Those were not matters in dispute in this case.
This evidence went beyond just saying, here's the steps the police took to investigate the case.
They went on to admit evidence that this is a forensically trained special interview process for children to go through
where special questions are asked, non-leading, non-suggestive questions.
So not only did she talk to a police officer, but she went through this special forensic process.
And during that interview process, an interdisciplinary team watched the interview
and could participate with the interviewer through an earpiece.
And that interdisciplinary team consisted of a prosecutor, police, DCYF, a victim witness...
That's accurately the process. What's wrong with that?
It's not relevant. It's not relevant and it's highly prejudicial.
It suggests that these allegations went through some special vetting process before the case was prosecuted.
When I was practicing, I would argue that that's not an investigation. That's trial prep.
Isn't that an argument that the defendant can raise?
It could. I hadn't thought about that.
But here, the way the evidence came in, it suggested this to the jury.
I would argue almost like suggesting to the jury that a complainant in a domestic case went and got a restraining order.
It's suggesting to the jury that the allegations passed through some special process, some unique process,
and it was carefully vetted before the case proceeded.
So your argument applies to any CAC interview, notwithstanding whether evidence of what was said
at the CAC interview was actually presented to the jury.
I think that would be different. I think if there was an issue about the content of the statements made during the CAC interview,
if the defense made an issue as to whether the CAC was suggestive,
then I think there is a basis to make it relevant for the jury to have an explanation as to what that process is.
Isn't that still relevant when you have inconsistent statements like we have here?
But the inconsistent statements here weren't inconsistent statements between what she said years earlier
and what her allegations were in the case at trial.
Not about what was said at the CAC interview.
How can the jury infer anything without knowing the content of the interview?
Just the fact that it took place without being told this is what the witness said.
On what basis wouldn't it be just sheer speculation for the jury to infer anything about that interview?
Well, the jury is told it has to look at and consider all the evidence in the case.
This evidence came in.
A jury, I think it would be perfectly reasonable to conclude that a jury would think that it went through this special process
because that was what was described to it, to the jury in evidence.
And what other conclusions would a jury draw from that special evidence?
The jury also heard that a prosecutor was in the room watching this,
knowing that, at least as the evidence was presented, that this was a special step that was taken
with a special neutral interviewer before the case proceeded to trial.
Before you sit down, is there anything in addition to what you've already said
with respect to harmless errors to the CAC interview?
You want to say?
I think it's the same as what I've already said.
I think collectively the evidence, given the weaknesses of the state's case,
that the error in admitting both pieces of evidence...
Is there anything unique about the CAC interview evidence that is relevant to the jury impasse?
I think it is, because the only thing the jury could have been struggling with
was the credibility of the evidence, and specifically the credibility of the complainant.
And this CAC interview process went specifically to her allegations
having gone through this special forensic process, so I think it is uniquely tied to that.
Thank you.
May it please the court, Adriana Makula, for the State of New Hampshire.
I'd like to start by talking about what this court has defined as the common plan or scheme evidence in Rule 404B.
And specifically to instances here where the state is trying to prove evidence of uncharged conduct
to show this common scheme or plan.
This court has held and meltured that the existence of a true plan is in the defendant's mind,
and that that includes both charged and uncharged crimes as stages in the defendant's plan
or execution of that plan, and that it is an objective standard.
So the proponent of the evidence must show that the defendant has a definite prior design or system,
which included the charged conduct and prior charged or uncharged conduct, depending on the case,
and that they are mutually dependent upon each other.
In this case, it doesn't matter whether the victim knew this was happening,
it doesn't matter how many times there was grooming or whatnot,
it just matters are these two acts or are these four acts mutually dependent upon each other,
and does that establish that there was an existence of a true plan in the defendant's mind?
This court has held time and again that proving what is in a defendant's mind can only be proved by circumstantial evidence.
And so here we don't need the victim to remember that this happened.
This court just needs to look at the evidence that was presented to the trial court before the trial
about what the testimony would be and determine if what happened in the victim's bedroom,
which was just that the defendant was in there a year before the assault,
if that was mutually, if their assaults themselves were mutually dependent on that.
The state's proffer to the trial court before trial is that it was.
And that is because this victim is young.
She was six or seven when the uncharged conduct occurred.
She was seven when the sexual assaults occurred.
And this behavior went along with the other behavior that the victim testified about that the defendant engaged in
that showed he was a father figure to her.
So the trial court accepted the proffers in its determination that the state had clear proof that this prior act occurred?
I think that has to be wrapped up in it only because the defendant did not challenge that there was clear proof
before the trial court and the court would have had to find that to admit evidence.
But I thought the hearsay statement came from an individual that denied that he ever witnessed this event happening.
So that is certainly what the defense attorney said.
However, the state never received a statement from the defense attorney about what that witness said.
So frankly, I'm not saying that the defense attorney is not telling the truth,
but the state never received any evidence of what this witness said to the defense attorney,
and that witness never denied saying that to the state.
And that was certainly never put before the jury.
The jury never heard from any other witness in that room or to this event.
So the state interviewed the woman that heard this statement?
Yes.
But they didn't interview the individual that made this observation?
I believe they did, but there was no information on the trial record or at this pre-trial record hearing
about what that witness said, what Chris said, other than he was interviewed.
I just don't know.
And I think part of the issue became here that the interviews of Ashley Williams and Chris
and other individuals were not recorded.
And so everybody was relying up until trial prep on whatever was in the police reports,
which I've not seen, the trial court did not see.
And then it was in the week leading up to the trial where the state started prepping its witnesses,
one of whom was Ashley Williams, that more detail about what happened the year prior came to light.
So I believe from what I remember reading this morning that the state interviewed Chris.
I don't know that it was clear to the trial court what Chris said to the state.
The defendant claimed through his attorney that Chris would deny it.
That statement was never provided to the state, from what I can tell.
However, the defendant is not challenging that admissibility of that hearsay statement on appeal.
It's challenging the testimony about this event which Ashley Williams witnessed,
which I get includes the statement, but really it seems to be that the meat of their argument on appeal
is that this was not part of some common planner scheme or at least the state did not prove that.
And even if it did establish that, that this was unfairly prejudicial
because she was testifying about how this worried her.
But again, if we're looking at the probative value of this evidence,
it was mutually, the sexual assaults that happened later were mutually dependent on this behavior
because it showed the jury that the defendant was able to operate as if he were her father
even though he was her stepfather.
It went along with her testimony about how he taught her how to fish, how to ride four-wheelers.
And then here is this evidence from Ashley Williams that when the six-year-old victim comes out
and is feeling unwell while her mother is asleep,
the defendant acts as her father and brings her into her room to put her back to bed.
Right. You can make that point without somebody saying that that conduct worried her.
But we don't know what was worrisome about that. No one asked her that at trial.
Sure, I guess it could be that she was worried that something nefarious was happening.
It could be that she was worried that the child was still sick
or that the child was throwing up in that bedroom
or that the defendant was struggling to put the child to bed.
We don't know what she meant by worse.
There's a lot of context of the case that would be hard to imagine
that a juror wouldn't make that influence, right?
I don't think it's hard to imagine that because the jury also heard that the victim loved this man
so much that even though she was not his biological daughter,
when her mother broke up with him, she demanded to go over there with her brother
to spend time with him. That was before the sexual assault occurred.
But she wanted to spend time with him.
So that already is confusing, I guess, to a jury when now you have a 16-year-old saying
that when I was 7, even though I willingly went over here time and again,
he sexually assaulted me. That is no different than that evidence.
Do you agree that the standard here is that the state has to show
a progressive, steady desensitization of the victim?
So what's the evidence of that here?
The evidence of that here is this coupled with all of the other testimony from the victim.
That this defendant acted as her father figure.
That's the desensitization.
That she would not think it was weird when we arrive at the first sexual assault
where she's asleep on her recliner, that this man gets into the recliner with her
and puts him on her stomach.
That to her because of the way he's behaved with her throughout her young life
does not concern her, does not cause her to protest, does not cause her to say
what is going on here, something's not right.
Obviously when the sexual assault occurs, I'm sure all of those feelings
and that 7-year-old body occurred.
But up until that point where he digitally penetrates her,
because of what he'd been doing with her as her father figure,
she was comfortable with all of that leading up to it.
And then that allows her behavior with that first sexual assault,
meaning not screaming, not stopping it, not telling anybody about it,
then allows the progression to occur to the next sexual assault
where now she's not sleeping.
He calls her into the bathroom with him while he's showering
and then gets out of the shower and has the second sexual assault occur,
which then progresses to the third one where they're in her bedroom,
but now he's using force to hold her down to perform the sexual assault.
Again, that clear progression happening because nothing bad happens to him
after that second sexual assault.
And she's still going over there to visit him.
Even though he's not her father, she could have easily said to her mother,
I don't want to go see a man who's not my father, but she doesn't do that.
And she doesn't do that because he's spent the time being her father figure
up until this point.
I don't want to make you jump around too much.
Sure.
And what your thoughts are regarding harmless error
and whether we can consider the jury deliberations in that context?
So I don't believe that I've read any harmless error cases
and I wrote the Boudreaux supplemental brief.
So I know I did a lot of research on harmless error cases in this jurisdiction.
I recall.
Thank you.
I know that the court has referenced jury deliberations,
although honestly I believe that you've referenced it when they've been short
as a reason to say the error was harmless
because the jury was able to come to a conclusion quickly, essentially.
I get that we have the opposite of that here.
However, having tried sexual assault cases myself
and having done a lot of sexual assault appeals,
I do not believe that this court should be in the business
of speculating about what jurors struggle with.
There are a lot of things that jurors could struggle with here
that have nothing to do with the testimony from Ashley Williams
and the testimony about the CAC.
And I'll explain what I mean more specifically,
but largely there are issues of did the state prove penetration
because that's a fact that the state has to prove
and do they believe that the seven-year-old
could really understand what that was.
That could have certainly been something they debated for five and a half hours.
They could have debated why a seven-year-old would lie
to a group of five adults about being sexually assaulted
or say I wasn't sexually assaulted at seven
and then come out later and say that she was at 15, 16 years old.
And I think that gets to why the CAC is relevant in this case.
It's not just relevant to show that this case was investigated.
It's relevant, one, to cut against the defense
that the victim just made this up during an argument with her mother.
Because why would she, in this CAC process,
that is about there's a forensically trained interview
who she's alone with who is asking her open-ended,
or non-leading questions?
At some level I think it's fair for anyone to infer from that
that she again repeated her disclosure
that she had repeated to her mother and her friend
in this sanitized space, meaning her mom is not there,
no one else is there, the police aren't there,
the prosecutor's not there, it's just a woman
she's never met before telling her I want you to tell me the truth.
But isn't that exactly the problem that the defense is raising here
is that that's the inference that you're attempting to establish
that during the CAC interview,
the victim actually repeated her story
in a way that was consistent with what you said on the stand?
Well, I think the jury could infer that from there
being no cross-examination of her with any statement she made
about the elements, whether that was through the CAC interview process
or not, and frankly, I agree that at trial
the defendant did not argue that this was not investigated properly
or that that was not their defense,
but that doesn't mean that it was agreed upon.
If the state had not called any detective
to explain this investigation process,
which is not some special process,
it's the process that the jury heard happens every single time
a child makes an allegation of some harm
or witnesses some harm.
The jury didn't think that this was exclusive to this victim,
but had the state not presented this evidence
it's entirely possible the defense could have pivoted
or included it in their defense theory
of she's in this argument with her mom,
she doesn't want to get in trouble,
so she lies about the defendant and look,
the police just took that as true
and did nothing to investigate it.
They did nothing and now the state is here
presenting it to you on this thread of evidence.
They certainly could have done that
and the state would not have had the opportunity to rebut that,
which is why this evidence is relevant.
This court has held in Roman
that the police investigation itself,
regardless of what it is, is relevant
to show that the police did their job
to collect the evidence for the state to use at the trial.
That's what the CAC is.
But in your brief, you say
the sergeant did not even testify
about any investigative steps
he took based on what he heard in the victim's CAC interview.
So this wasn't portrayed
as part of the police investigation, was it?
Well, what I meant by that was
there was nothing he learned from the CAC interview
that he chose to follow up on.
But in talking about the CAC
he also talked about the other people he interviewed.
He interviewed her mother,
he interviewed her friend,
he interviewed the defendant,
and this is part of my harmless error argument,
the defendant does not deny
sexually assaulting the seven-year-old.
The defendant just says,
well, I guess I should
not be around her and let this blow over
because clearly something wrong has happened here.
These are all investigative steps
that the state took.
That's a statement he made to the mother,
not the detective.
Right, oh, I'm sorry, yes, sorry.
But he interviewed the mother
and said that he would have received that information
from interviewing the mother.
So my point in making that in the brief
was to show that the CAC
did not lead to any additional investigative steps alone
but that he had taken investigative steps
into the case that he would in any other case
which was including interviewing people,
interviewing the victim
through this process that is set up
for children everywhere
and then collecting any other evidence
of which there was none in this particular case.
I know I sort of have veered away from your question
about considering the length of jury deliberations
but I do think we really have no evidence
about what they deliberated about.
We don't even really have a question
other than the question saying we're at an impasse.
We don't know what they were struggling with
and so to assume that they were struggling
with the victim's credibility
and that then therefore
Ashley Williams testimony and the CAC interview
must have pushed them over the hump is unfair.
But can we come to the conclusion
that an impasse at least suggests
that the state's evidence wasn't overwhelming?
I don't think you can
because they reached a guilty verdict
and so just because it took them
five and a half hours to do so
doesn't mean that the evidence was not overwhelming
especially because
my understanding at least of how the court
views this standard,
this harmless error standard
is you're looking at all of the evidence
that was admitted at the trial
not replacing a juror as you would be
if there was some sort of claim
that the evidence was insufficient
or that the evidence
was against the weight of justice
or against the weight of the evidence at trial.
You are just looking at what was presented
and using the nine factors
in Boudreaux
and then any other factors that are not listed
to determine if it's harmless error.
I think there was
sufficient or there was overwhelming evidence
in this case because you don't just have
the victim's testimony.
You have the defendant making these comments
that are not denials.
You have the victim's friend
and her mother talking about the disclosures
that she made to them and how emotional they were
and how they sort of just came out of the blue.
You have evidence
that she was young
when this occurred and that she did not
want to tell when she was seven
because she did not want to harm her brother.
All of that is
an explanation for how it was
that she would be sexually assaulted at seven
and then not report until she was 16.
I assume my red light is on.
I will rest on my briefs. Thank you.
Thank you very much, attorney P-1.
A few points.
Just because a guilty verdict has been entered
doesn't mean the evidence was overwhelming.
I mean that's
that the state's burden
is beyond a reasonable doubt
and all of the factors that the court has been asking
us about relate to
assessing whether the state has met that burden
of harmlessness.
I want to
just reference
the prior act evidence
and just
pull out from the
transcript for the court
what the witness actually said
pages 189 to 90.
She,
the witness is saying that
client was in the room, Mr.
Garot was in the room, a significant amount of time
that I was worried
and then
she says
in her testimony, he then came
right out so he was alert
that we and then the prosecutor
cuts her off and says, well
we don't want to speculate, right?
That's the testimony
that came out about that prior act.
The state's argument
Justice Gould, I think you
were correct. The state's argument
about why the CAC interview
was relevant
proves
or establishes and demonstrates
that they were using that CAC
interview process evidence
to bolster the credibility
of the allegations.
That's the problem here
and in fact in opening statement
and closing argument
the prosecutor explains
this was not a police station
it's a forensically trained
interviewer, not a police officer
it's a place where the child
can tell their story
without any leading questions
without someone putting words in their mouth
that's in the opening
in closing the prosecutor
goes back to it, why would she lie
to the interviewer?
The prosecutor then further says
she was consistent
each time she made statements
to the CAC, to her mother
to her friend. There was an objection
to that comment which was sustained
because there was no evidence
about what the complainant
said in those interviews but the prosecutor
went right back to it. There
has been no time where she said
it did not happen.
The state used the
CAC as
a way to argue
that the allegations the complainant
made then went through this
special forensic process
and they're more credible now.
But there were moments when she said
it did not happen.
Correct, and that was much earlier on.
The court did instruct the jury
my memory is I can't find it but the court did
instruct the jury at some point about the CAC
didn't it?
I don't believe
so I may be misremembering
something.
The trial court
my recollections
the jury was
instructed to disregard the prosecutor's
comment, correct?
The one comment where she said
where the prosecutor said
she meaning the complainant was consistent
each time she talked to
and then that was sustained
and there was an instruction to disregard it
because there was no evidence about what the complainant
said during those times.
But then the prosecutor went back
and said there has been
no time where she hasn't said
this hasn't happened. Thank you.
Case submitted.

initial_prompt: This is a New Hampshire Supreme Court oral argument. Preserve legal terminology, party names, docket numbers, and court names. Likely speakers include justices and counsel.
text:
This is case 2024, 0505, state of New Hampshire versus Max Giroux.

Good morning for the record.

Justice Will is unable to be with us physically present today, but he is going to participate in our deliberations having reviewed recording of the argument.

Tony Feeland.

Good morning. I have reserved two minutes.

May it please the court? My name is Pamela Feeland. I'm here today on behalf of Mr. Giroux.

There are three points I'd like to address this morning.

First, the prior uncharged and isolated act was not part of a clearly discernible plan to groom the complainant in this case.

Two, evidence about the child advocacy interview process improperly bolstered the credibility of the complainant's allegations.

And lastly, the error in admitting both types of evidence was not harmless beyond a reasonable doubt.

Regarding the prior act evidence, discreet and isolated acts with no demonstrated connection to the charged acts are not acts that are part of a calculated plan to commit later assaults and are not admissible as grooming evidence.

This court has been very clear in all the cases it has issued addressing the admissibility of alleged grooming evidence.

There has to be a sequence of acts that demonstrate a discernible plan on the behalf of the defendant.

That a sequence of acts resembles a design when examined in retrospect is not enough.

The prior conduct must be intertwined with what follows such that the prior conduct and the charged act were mutually dependent.

That's a quote from the State v. Melcher case.

The defining characteristics that this court has focused on in these cases involving alleged grooming evidence include whether there are more than one act,

what is the temporal relationship between the single act or acts and the charged act.

Here, there was at least a year period of time between one single act that was admitted as grooming evidence and the charged acts that came later.

Interestingly enough, in all the grooming evidence cases that I've looked at from this court, the complainant in the case is the one who has talked about in testimony

the alleged grooming acts and how it interrelated with the charged acts.

In this case, that is not the evidence. The complainant in this case wasn't even asked about the prior act or how she viewed it in relation to the charged act or otherwise.

So we don't know whether she remembered that ever happening?

Correct. There was no evidence that the charged acts hinged on would not have happened or were mutually dependent on this single isolated act that a third party testified about.

And again, there was no particular temporal connection between the charged act, acts, and that prior act.

Interestingly enough, the state didn't even articulate in arguments to the jury how that prior act made it possible for the charged acts to happen,
